{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 0.7,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "R1.7",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To scope 'Improve this draft,' should I: (A) perform light copyediting only (grammar, clarity, concision) while keeping structure, claims, and citations largely unchanged; or (B) perform a substantive revision that may reframe the contribution, reorganize sections, tighten claim–evidence alignment, and add/adjust citations)? Please choose A or B.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Reframing the thesis and gap will make the core contribution and its context clearer without changing the underlying results.",
    "R1.2": "The chain from problem to method to results to implications should be checked for coherence and unsupported leaps.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about bias reductions and practical impact should be explicitly tied to the described experiments and metrics.",
    "R1.5": "Section-level ordering can be adjusted to strengthen the narrative arc from motivation through method, evaluation, and implications.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level copyediting will improve clarity, concision, tone, and readability.",
    "R1.7": "Core references (e.g., propensity score foundations and IHDP sources) should be properly cited and attribution made precise.",
    "RX.1": "Edits should surface uncertainties and limitations rather than implying unwarranted generality.",
    "RX.2": "Language must avoid overstating contributions beyond what the reported evidence supports.",
    "RX.3": "All work will remain within improving this draft without adding ungrounded content or new empirical claims.",
    "RX.4": "Wording should minimize risk of harmful misinterpretation in policy settings by clarifying scope and applicability.",
    "RX.5": "Changes should be traceable to specific sentences and supported by identifiable sources for any added citations."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To scope 'Improve this draft,' should I: (A) perform light copyediting only (grammar, clarity, concision) while keeping structure, claims, and citations largely unchanged; or (B) perform a substantive revision that may reframe the contribution, reorganize sections, tighten claim–evidence alignment, and add/adjust citations)? Please choose A or B.

rationale.R1.1: Reframing the thesis and gap will make the core contribution and its context clearer without changing the underlying results.
rationale.R1.2: The chain from problem to method to results to implications should be checked for coherence and unsupported leaps.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about bias reductions and practical impact should be explicitly tied to the described experiments and metrics.
rationale.R1.5: Section-level ordering can be adjusted to strengthen the narrative arc from motivation through method, evaluation, and implications.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level copyediting will improve clarity, concision, tone, and readability.
rationale.R1.7: Core references (e.g., propensity score foundations and IHDP sources) should be properly cited and attribution made precise.
rationale.RX.1: Edits should surface uncertainties and limitations rather than implying unwarranted generality.
rationale.RX.2: Language must avoid overstating contributions beyond what the reported evidence supports.
rationale.RX.3: All work will remain within improving this draft without adding ungrounded content or new empirical claims.
rationale.RX.4: Wording should minimize risk of harmful misinterpretation in policy settings by clarifying scope and applicability.
rationale.RX.5: Changes should be traceable to specific sentences and supported by identifiable sources for any added citations.
